coredata.dc:title: Improved photocatalytic performance for selective oxidation of amines to imines on graphitic carbon nitride/bismuth tungstate heterojunctions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Aili
Lei, Hua
Wang, Zhongsen
Dong, Xiaoping
coredata.dc:description: Imine compounds are vital intermediates in organic synthesis, and their cost-effective and green preparation has inspired numerous interests. Herein, we employ an environmentally friendly photocatalysis strategy to selectively oxidize amines to obtain imines. The heterojunction photocatalysts of graphitic carbon nitride/bismuth tungstate (CN/BW) were fabricated by hydrothermal growth of bismuth tungstate on the surface of graphitic carbon nitride. Characterization results demonstrate that the composites have been well composited and show improved separation ability of photo-generated charge carriers due to the formed type II band alignment. In comparison to the individual CN and BW, the heterojunctions exhibit apparent enhanced activity for the selective oxidation of amines to imines using O2 as the oxidant under irradiation of visible light. The optimized CN/BW heterojuntion with 75% BW content presents the high conversion (~98%) and selectivity (~98%) in 3 h oxidative coupling reaction of benzylamine. Furthermore, this heterojunction photocatalyst also has excellent cycling stability, as well as good catalytic performance for various benzylamine derivatives. On the basis of experimental results, the transfer pathway of photo-excited electrons and holes in the heterojunction photocatalyst was illustrated and a possible mechanism for the subsequent selective oxidation of amines to imines was proposed. This work provides an economical, sustainable and thus green process for the amine transformation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphitic carbon nitride
Bismuth tungstate
Heterojunction
Photocatalysis
Oxidative coupling
Amines
Imines
Molecular oxygen

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedures for the oxidative coupling of amines
Photocatalytic performance for selective oxidation of amines

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of CN (a), BW (b) and CN/BW (c, d); EDS elemental mappings of CN/BW (The scale bar is 10 μm).
TEM (a, b) and HRTEM (c) images of CN/BW; XRD patterns (d), FTIR spectra (e) and UV–vis DRS (f) of CN, BW and CN/BW samples.
XPS spectra of CN/BW: Survey (a), C 1s (b), N 1s (c), Bi 4f (d), W 4f (e) and O 1s (f).
EIS Nyquist plots (a) and photo-current responses (b) of CN, BW and CN/BW samples.
Conversion and selectivity of CN/BW for five successive runs of photocatalytic oxidation of benzylamine to N-benzylidene benzylamine.
Tauc plots (a, b), Mott-Schottky curves (c, d) and XPS VB spectra (e, f) of CN (a, c, e) and BW (b, d, f); Schematic diagram for the band alignment (g).
Conversions of benzylamine with various scavengers (a) and under different atmospheres (b); possible photocatalytic mechanism of CN/BW for selective oxidation of amines to imines (c).
Control experiments for photocatalytic oxidation of benzylamine to N-benzylidenebenzylamine under visible light irradiation. a

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Reaction conditions: benzylamine (0.2 mmol), catalyst (20 mg), solvent (5 mL), O2 (1 atm), λ > 420 nm visible light.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Oxidative coupling of various amines to imines catalyzed with CN/BW(75%). a

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Reaction conditions: benzylamine (0.2 mmol), catalyst (20 mg), solvent (CH3CN, 5 mL), O2 (1 atm), λ > 420 nm visible light.

head.ce:title: Improved photocatalytic performance for selective oxidation of amines to imines on graphitic carbon nitride/bismuth tungstate heterojunctions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, 928 Second Avenue, Xiasha Higher Education Zone, Hangzhou 310018, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, 928 Second Avenue, Xiasha Higher Education Zone, Hangzhou 310018, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Imine compounds are vital intermediates in organic synthesis, and their cost-effective and green preparation has inspired numerous interests. Herein, we employ an environmentally friendly photocatalysis strategy to selectively oxidize amines to obtain imines. The heterojunction photocatalysts of graphitic carbon nitride/bismuth tungstate (CN/BW) were fabricated by hydrothermal growth of bismuth tungstate on the surface of graphitic carbon nitride. Characterization results demonstrate that the composites have been well composited and show improved separation ability of photo-generated charge carriers due to the formed type II band alignment. In comparison to the individual CN and BW, the heterojunctions exhibit apparent enhanced activity for the selective oxidation of amines to imines using O2 as the oxidant under irradiation of visible light. The optimized CN/BW heterojuntion with 75% BW content presents the high conversion (~98%) and selectivity (~98%) in 3 h oxidative coupling reaction of benzylamine. Furthermore, this heterojunction photocatalyst also has excellent cycling stability, as well as good catalytic performance for various benzylamine derivatives. On the basis of experimental results, the transfer pathway of photo-excited electrons and holes in the heterojunction photocatalyst was illustrated and a possible mechanism for the subsequent selective oxidation of amines to imines was proposed. This work provides an economical, sustainable and thus green process for the amine transformation.

ce:section.ce:para: Imines and their derivatives, a functional group of chemicals that contain a CN group, are vital intermediates for the synthesis of various fine chemicals, biological nitrogenous intermediates, and molecular motors, etc. [1–3]. Due to their wide application in many fields, synthesis of imines has been gaining tremendous attentions in recent years. And, multiple strategies have been successfully employed for synthesis of imines, for instance, condensation of aldehydes/ketones and amines [4,5], oxidative coupling of alcohols and amines [6,7], and direct oxidation of amines [8,9]. In the condensation reaction between aldehydes/ketones and amines, Lewis acid catalysts and an expensive dehydrating agent are required, therefore limiting the practical application of the method. Meanwhile, for the approach of oxidative coupling of alcohols and amines, the low oxidation activity of alcohol is the main and insurmountable problem. Alternatively, the direct oxidation of amines has become a more attractive strategy.

So far, a series of catalyst systems for amine oxidation based on transition-metals (e.g. Ru, Au, V, Cu, Mn and Ir) have been reported [9–14]. However, the utilization of metal catalysts will result in cost problems and metal contaminants in the final product. Furthermore, oxidants employed in some oxidation procedures, such as N-tert-butylphenylsulfinimidoyl chloride and 2-iodoxybenzoic acid, are usually hazardous [9]. From the viewpoints of economical and environmental concerns, direct oxidation of amines to imines with molecular oxygen driven by cheap heterogeneous catalysts is consequently desirable.

Semiconductor photocatalysis, using natural solar energy and environmental friendly oxidants (e.g. molecular oxygen and photo-generated holes), has been regarded as a green oxidation process for synthesis of various value-added organics [15–17], including for the selective oxidation of amines to imines [18–33]. For example, Zhao and co-works reported that photocatalytic oxidation of amines to imines by TiO2 under UV irradiation with high selectivity [18]. They also discovered that oxidized amines to corresponding imines using TiO2 under visible light, which was due to benzylamine adsorbed onto the surface of TiO2 could extend light absorption into the visible range [19]. Similarly, Tanaka et al. found the visible-light induced selective oxidation of amines to imines on Nb2O5 with a wide band gap of 3.2 eV [20]. Wang et al. have synthesized Au nanoclusters supported on TiO2 which the turnover frequency (TOF) reaches up to 1522 h−1 in the 4-methylbenzylamine oxidation [25]. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are promising materials for photocatalytic applications because of their structure and properties with adjustable properties, and more importantly, various photo-induced charge/energy transfer processes in MOFs [21,28,29]. Jiang et al. showed a new strategy for integrating photocatalytic H2 production with selective benzylamine oxidation on MOF composites under light illumination [28]. Xing et al. prepared a novel three-dimensional In-MOF with high activity for benzylamine oxidation, and it had a wide range of absorption in the visible region due to the introduction of a photosensitive organic linker [29]. Notwithstanding numerous efforts, it is still a challenge to achieve high efficiency for selective oxidation of amines to imines using inexpensive green photocatalysts.

Among these photocatalysts, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has stimulated tremendous research interests because of its unique physicochemical properties, such as, high stability, metal-free composition, appropriate band gap (~2.7 eV) for visible light absorption and suitable band-edge positions (ECB = −1.3 eV, EVB = 1.4 eV) [34,35]. Similar to conventional photocatalysts, however, the g-C3N4 also suffers the rapid recombination of photo-excited electron-hole pairs, which results in the suppression photocatalytic activity. Development of g-C3N4 based materials with high charge separation efficiency for photocatalytic applications, for instance water-splitting for hydrogen evolution, synthesis of solar fuels from photocatalytic CO2 reduction, photocatalytic degradation of environmental pollutants and organic synthesis, is therefore of immense research interest in recent years [35–38]. In particular, fabrication of heterojunction photocatalysts during coupling g-C3N4 with other semiconductors is one of the most effective strategies to promote the charge separation, for example g-C3N4/sulfides [36,37], g-C3N4/oxides [38,39], g-C3N4/Bi based materials [40–42] and g-C3N4/carbon quantum dots [43–45].

As one of the Aurivillius layered compounds, Bi2WO6 consists of alternate stacking of [Bi2O2] slabs and corner-shared WO6 octahedral slabs. Besides the excellent visible light absorption, its internal polarization field can spatially separate photo-generated charges [46,47]. The g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 heterojunction has been demonstrated to possess excellent activities for various photocatalytic applications [46–48]. However, as far as we know, the g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 photocatalyst has not been used for oxidation of aromatic amines to imines. Motivated by these effective attempts, we herein employed the g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 heterojunction to catalyze the selective oxidation reaction of amines to imines using the green oxidant of molecular oxygen. The obtained heterojunction of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 has type II band alignment that drove photo-induced electrons and holes to be respectively accumulated on different semiconductors. Accordingly, compared with the individual g-C3N4 and Bi2WO6, the heterojunction presented superior conversion (98%) and high selectivity (98%) for the conversion of various amines to their corresponding imines under visible light irradiation. Furthermore, the photocatalyst was easily recycled and retained excellent activity in this reaction system.

Acetonitrile (CH3CN, 99.5%), benzylamine (C7H9N, 99%) and other substrate (99%) were provided by Shanghai Aladdin industrial corporation (China). Bismuth (III) nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, 98%) and melamine (C3H6N6, 99%) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. (China). Sodium tungstate dehydrate (Na2WO4·2H2O, 96%) was obtained from Hangzhou Gaojing Chemistry Co. Ltd. (China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was prepared from a Millipore Milli-Q purification unit.

Graphitic carbon nitride was prepared using melamine as precursor according to the synthetic procedure in our previous literatures [44]. In a typical procedure, 16 g of melamine was placed in a semi-closed corundum crucible that was subsequently heated in a muffle furnace at 520 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting solid g-C3N4 (marked as CN) was ground and collected.

Heterojunction photocatalysts of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 (CN/BW) was synthesized though a hydrothermal method. Firstly, 1 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved in 30 mL glacial acetic acid under stirring. In the meantime, 0.5 mmol Na2WO4·2H2O and a certain amount of CN were dispersed into 10 mL distilled water. After dropwise adding the Bi(NO3)3·5H2O solution into the above suspension under vigorous stirring for 30 min, the obtained mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and treated at 180 °C for 12 h. Ultimately, the resultant was collected by centrifugation, following the repeated water washing for several times and the drying treatment at 80 °C for 12 h. If without specially pointing out, the CN/BW means a composite photocatalyst with a 75% mass ratio of BW/CN in this work. Similarly, the pure Bi2WO6 (BW) was prepared according to the above method without addition of CN.

Morphologies and elemental distributions of photocatalysts were analyzed by a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Quanta 250 FEG) equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken on a transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100 F) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Crystal structure of photocatalysts was identified by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) on a DX-2700 diffractometer (Dandong Haoyuan Instrument Co. Ltd. China). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were tested on a Nicolet Avatar 370 spectrophotometer with a KBr pellet. UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were carried out on a Shimadzu 2450 UV–vis spectrometer with an integrating sphere using BaSO4 as the reference. Data of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were collected on a VG K-ALPHA instrument using Al Kα monochromatic source. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength at 340 nm by Edinburgh FL/FS900 spectrophotometer. Electrochemical performance was characterized by a CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for the oxidative coupling of amines
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic performance for amine selective oxidation was estimated in a closed container under oxygen atmosphere using a 300 W xenon lamp (HSX-F300, Beijing NBet) with a 420 nm cutoff filter as the light source. In each experiment, 20 mg of photocatalyst and 0.2 mmol of amine were added into 5 mL of acetonitrile in a 50 mL Pyrex vessel with an oxygen bag, and then the mixture was stirred for 30 min in dark to make adsorption equilibrium. After the reaction, the crude mixtures were analyzed using a gas chromatograph (GC, Shimadzu) with a flame ionization detector (FID).

The photocatalyst of CN/BW was obtained by the in-situ hydrothermal growth of BW on the CN surface. As shown in the SEM image of the pristine CN (Fig. 1 a), large and irregular particles with smooth surface are observed. The hydrothermally prepared BW (Fig. 1b) has relatively small size, hundreds of nanometers in lateral size and tens of nanometers in thickness. After precipitation of BW on CN, the surface of CN became rough (Fig. 1c). From the high-magnification SEM image in Fig. 1d, we can clearly find amounts of BW nanoflakes cover on the CN surface, whose size and morphology are similar to the individual BW. To further demonstrate the well coupling of CN and BW, EDS examination was performed, and its elemental mapping images are presented in Fig. 1e. The distributions of C and N elements in the CN phase are consistent with the three elements (Bi, W and O) of BW phase, indicating that the BW nanoflakes are uniformly deposited onto the surface of CN.

Fig. 2 a-c depicts TEM images of CN/BW sample in different amplifications, which confirm that aggregated BW nanoflakes modify on CN surface. Clear lattice fringes are observed in the flake-like phase from the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 2c) that are attributed to the (1 1 3) and (2 2 0) facets of russellite BW phase, respectively with 0.31 nm and 0.19 nm lattice spacing. In addition, some low crystallization level or amorphous zones are also found. It should be ascribed to the CN phase. The structure of photocatalysts is characterized by XRD technology, as revealed in Fig. 2d. A peak at ~27° is observed in the CN pattern that represents the interlayer ordered arrangement of layers. The CN sample also shows a peak at ~13°, reflecting the intra-planar regular arrangement of tri-s-triazine units [43]. The very weak intensity of this diffraction suggests the intra-planar non-crystal nature that is in accordance with the HRTEM result. For the pure BW sample, six peaks are checked in the 2θ range of 20–70°. These diffractions are matched with those of russellite phase BW (JCPDS 73-1126), respectively corresponding to (1 1 3), (2 0 0), (2 2 0), (3 1 3), (2 2 6) and (4 0 0) facets. The CN/BW sample only exhibits the diffractions of BW, and the diffractions of CN are not able to be checked. It may be attributed to the weak diffraction intensity of CN and the similar 2θ value for the (0 0 2) diffraction of CN and the (1 1 3) peak of BW. This phenomenon widely exists in g-C3N4 based composites [49].

Nevertheless, we can determine the well coupling of CN and BW by FTIR measurements (Fig. 2e). In the FTIR spectrum of BW, the absorptions at ~3426 cm−1, ~730 cm−1 and ~580 cm−1 correspond to the OH stretch vibration in adsorbed water, the BiO and WO stretching modes, respectively [49]. For the pure CN sample, characteristic absorption bands are observed, including the ~3165 cm−1 peak for NH vibration from the terminal amino groups in CN, several absorptions in the range of 1600–1200 cm−1 for the conjugated CN based heterocycles, and the ~810 cm−1 band for the breathing mode of tri-s-triazine unit [45]. These absorption bands are all presented in the CN/BW sample, revealing the successful construction of CN/BW heterojunction. The optical absorption properties of these samples were examined by UV–vis DRS. As displayed in Fig. 2f, the pristine CN and BW have strong absorption in visible light region, and their absorption onsets respectively appear at the wavelengths of ~480 nm and ~430 nm. For the CN/BW composite, the spectrum shows a two-stage absorption edge, where the former results from the CN absorption and the latter is from the BW.

The compositions and chemical environments of elements in the CN/BW sample were investigated by XPS technique. Five constituent elements, C and N in the CN, Bi, W and O in the BW, are all presented in the survey spectrum (Fig. 3 a). The high resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) is divided into four peaks at binding energies of 284.6, 286.1, 288.1 and 289.3 eV. The peak at a binding energy of 284.6 eV corresponds to the adventitious carbon contamination (CC/CC), and the absorptions at 286.1 eV and 289.3 eV are related to the C atoms in COH and CO groups, which may be from the adsorbed acetate ion that was used in the synthesis of BW. In addition, the peak at 288.1 eV is ascribed to the sp2 C atoms in N-containing aromatic skeleton rings (NCN) [45]. As illustrated in Fig. 3c, three signals in the N 1s spectrum agree with three groups in g-C3N4, 398.7 eV, 399.5 eV and 400.8 eV respectively for the sp2 N atoms in the conjugated CN ring, the N(C)3 and NH groups respectively [44]. The Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 absorption bands in the Bi 4f spectrum (Fig. 3d) locate at the binding energy of 165.3 eV and 160.1 eV, which are assigned to the Bi3+ ions in Bi2WO6 structure [50]. Fig. 3e displays the W 4f spectrum of the CN/BW sample, where the W 4f5/2 and W 4f7/2 signals are centered at 38.4 eV and 36.3 eV. These values also are in accordance with the W6+ in Bi2WO6 [46]. Similarly, the O 1s spectrum presents the peak at 531.5 eV that is related to the O atom in Bi2WO6 [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance for selective oxidation of amines
ce:section.ce:para: Benzylamine was initially selected as the model substrate to investigate the photocatalytic performance of CN/BW in oxidative coupling of amines to imines under oxygen atmosphere (1 atm). Prior to the photocatalytic reaction, the suspension was stirred in the dark and the adsorption/desorption equilibrium was reached after 30 min (~14% benzylamine was adsorbed, as seen in Fig. S1). All controlled trials are shown in Table 1 . In the absence of light source or catalyst, very low conversion is observed, which confirms that the oxidation of benzylamine is a visible-light-driven reaction, and catalyst has a catalytic effect (Table 1, entry 1–2). The pure CN and BW exhibit general photocatalytic activities, and the conversion of benzylamine to N-benzylidenebenzylamine is 27% and 52% respectively, after 6 h irradiation (Table 1, entry 3–4). Compared to CN and BW, the CN/BW heterojunctions with different BW weight percentages show significantly improved conversion (84%–98%) and high selectivity (83%–98%) with O2 at room temperature (Table 1, entry 5–7). Besides N-benzylidenebenzylamine in products, a small amount of benzaldehyde as a by-product is also checked. Among these CN/BW samples, the photocatalyst with 75% BW content has the highest photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, when the lighting time is shortened to 3 h, this CN/BW maintains the high conversion efficiency (98%), and meanwhile presents an enhanced selectivity (98%). This result suggests that the long reaction time would result in the over oxidation of benzylamine. Within 3 h reaction, the reaction conversion of CN/BW with 75% BW is ~8.9 times and ~ 3.2 times that of the native CN (11%) and the pure BW (31%) (Table 1, entry 8–10). In addition, the clear saturated lime water that was connected to the photocatalytic reactor did not become turbid (Fig. S2). However, once replacing benzylamine with a dye solution, the clear limewater immediately became turbid. This result confirms that the benzylamine was not over oxidized to CO2.

The effect of solvent was studied and the results are shown in entry 11–12 of Table 1, and it is found that acetonitrile (CH3CN) is the best medium for the solvent tested. Additionally, the photocatalytic behaviors of CN/BW heterojunctions were also performed in air atmosphere, in which the photocatalytic performance is inferior to the oxygen condition (Table S1). Thus, we chose the photocatalyst of CN/BW with 75% BW, the reaction time of 3 h, the solvent of CH3CN and the oxygen atmosphere as the optimized reaction conditions for the following studies. We also compared the photocatalytic activity of the CN/BW composite for oxidative coupling of benzylamine under the optimized conditions with those reported photocatalysts in literatures (Table S2). Obviously, the CN/BW photocatalyst in this study has a much higher conversion and TOF value than those of the reported photocatalysts [19,21–23,26,27,32,33].

As a heterogeneous photocatalyst, the CN/BW heterojunction photocatalyst can be easily separated from the reaction solution by filtration or centrifugation. On the basis of practical application, the photochemical stability of composite photocatalyst was tested by recycling experiments. In every experiment, the photocatalyst was recycled by centrifugation and washed several times with ethanol and acetonitrile. After five continuous cycles (Fig. 5 ), only a <10% decrease in conversion is observed, while the catalytic selectivity remains greater than 98%.

Under the optimized reaction conditions, we further studied the photocatalytic performance of CN/BW photocatalyst for oxidative coupling of various benzylamine derivatives, and the results are shown in Table 2 . A series of benzylamines can be readily oxidized to form corresponding imines in good conversion (72%–99%) and high selectivity (90%–99%). It is found that electron-withdrawing (OCH3, CH3) and electron-donating (F, Cl) groups on the benzylamine do not affect the conversion significantly, but slightly changed the selectivity rates. It reveals that the electronic effect has no obvious effect on the reaction conversion. Notably, the conversion of (4-tert-butylphenyl)methanamine into its corresponding imine is significantly lower than those of other para-substituted benzylamine, indicating that the steric effect maybe have an effect on this reaction. This steric effect is further demonstrated by the decreased conversion of m-chlorobenzylamine and o-chlorobenzylamine in comparison to the p-chlorobenzylamine. From the above experimental results, the effect of the electronic effect on the reaction is not obvious, and further research is needed.

Fig. 6 a and b illustrate Tauc plots of CN and BW. Their corresponding band gap (E g) values are 2.58 and 2.70 eV. Electrochemical Mott-Schottky (MS) measurement is employed to determine the semiconductive type and flat band (namely Fermi energy level, E F) potential. As shown in Fig. 6c and d, both CN and BW are n-type semiconductors, and the potentials of their E F are respectively −1.19 V and −1.05 V vs. Ag/AgCl for CN and BW. They can be converted into the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) potential using the blow formula:
(1)
E RHE
=
E
Ag / AgCl
+ 0.198 V + 0.0592 pH
where the pH value of the Na2SO4 electrolyte is 6.8 [52]. Thus, the calculated E F potentials of CN and BW should be −0.59 V and −0.45 V vs. RHE. In addition, the relative potential of valence band (VB) vs. Fermi energy level was obtained by XPS VB spectra (Fig. 6e and f), where the estimated value is 1.44 eV for CN and 2.14 eV for BW, respectively. Based on these obtained potential values of CN and BW and their E g values as well, we schematically plot the energy band diagrams of CN and BW in Fig. 6g.

In order to clarify the mechanism of photocatalytic oxidation coupling of benzylamine, the main active species in the reaction process were determined by quenching experiments. The effect of adding various quenchers, 3 mM triethanolamine (TEOA) for h+, 3 mM K2S2O8 for e−, 3 mM p-benzoquinone (BQ) for
O2 −, and 3 mM tert-butanol (TBA) for
OH, on the photocatalytic performance of the CN/BW was studied and the results are shown in Fig. 7 a. The addition of BQ into the reaction system induces a sharp drop of conversion to 21%. When using K2S2O8 or TEOA, the photocatalytic performance also decreases obviously. However, the activity has no obvious change as adding TBA. Based on these trapping experiments of active species, we can infer that superoxide radicals are the active species in the oxidation of amines to imines, and photo-generated electrons and holes are initial drivers for this reaction. In addition, the photocatalytic oxidative coupling of benzylamine has been carried out under different atmosphere (Fig. 7b). The conversion of benzylamine in N2 atmosphere decreases significantly from 41% (in air) to 14%, while it evidently increases to 98% when the reaction is conducted in O2 atmosphere for 3 h. The results demonstrate that the presence of O2 is essential for the oxidative coupling of benzylamine in the present system and the increased O2 concentration can accelerate the reaction rate so much. To acquire more information on the mechanism in photocatalytic redox reaction, the relative oxidation rates of para-substituted benzylamines (OCH3, CH3, H, Cl and F groups) were investigated. A reasonably near-linear relationship between log (k X/k H) and the Brown-Okamoto constant σ was established for the oxidation of para-substituted benzylamine, as shown in Fig. S3. The slope of the plot (ρ value) is negative, indicating that the reaction probably occurs via a carbocation intermediate species [8]. And it indicates the existence of an electronic effect in this reaction that the oxidation rate of amines with electron-donating substituents precedes much more efficiently than those amines with electron-withdrawing substituents.

Based on the above experimental results, the proposed mechanism for photocatalytic oxidation of benzylamine to imine by CN/BW is shown in Fig. 7c. The electrons on the VB of BW and CN are excited by the visible light irradiation and transit the band gap to their corresponding CB, meanwhile leaving holes on VB. Due to the formation of type II heterojunction of the CN/BW composite, these photo-generated charge carriers are effectively separated via the interfacial migration. As a consequence, the photo-excited electrons are accumulated on the CB of BW and the photo-induced holes are collected on the VB of CN, respectively. The benzylamine molecules adsorbed on CN sites would be oxidized by the holes to produce the benzylamine radical cation. Meanwhile, the photo-generated electron collected on the CB of BW reduces molecular oxygen to produce superoxide radical anions. Subsequently, the
O2 − abstracts a proton and a hydrogen atom form the benzylamine radical cation forming the intermediates of NH-imine and H2O2. The H2O2 intermediate in our reaction system was also detected by the spectrophotometric N,N-Diethyl-p-phenylenediamine (DPD) method as shown in Fig. S4 [24]. It is worth noting that the NH-imine species is very reactive in the photocatalytic process. Finally, the intermediate is prone to nucleophilic attack by the amine to afford the final coupled product with releasing a NH3 molecule.

We have successfully fabricated an efficient transporting pathway for photo-induced charge carriers in the CN/BW heterojunction photocatalyst by forming the interface of g-C3N4 and Bi2WO6. This photocatalyst displays the activity improvement for the oxidative coupling of amines to obtain their corresponding imines. The benzylamine conversion achieved by the optimized CN/BW heterojunction with 75% BW content is ~8.9 times and ~3.2 times that of the individual CN and BW, respectively. Furthermore, a high selectivity to N-benzylidenebenzylamine (98%) is simultaneously obtained in this photocatalytic system. Impressively, this heterojunction photocatalyst has a superior stability for long-time use, and can drive the conversion of various amines to selective formation of their corresponding imines. Such attractive performance demonstrates that this CN/BW heterojunction can be used as efficient and green photocatalyst for selective oxidation of amines to imines.
Declaration of Interest Statement

There is no conflict of interest between all authors and all authors declare that: (1) no support, financial or otherwise, has been received from any organization that may have an interest in the submitted work; and (2) there are no other relationships or activities that could appear to have influenced the submitted work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported from the National Key R&D Program of China (2017YFE0127400) and the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LY17B010004).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2019.10.060.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthetic aspects of metal-catalyzed oxidations of amines and related reactions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.I. Murahashi, Synthetic aspects of metal-catalyzed oxidations of amines and related reactions, Angew. Chem. 34 (1995) 2443-2465.
